title: Segmentation, Targeting & Positioning
simple_html:
No company can be everything to everyone. STP — Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning — is the strategic process for choosing which customers to serve and how to win in their minds. It is the heart of marketing strategy and the link between understanding buyers and designing the marketing mix.

The three steps

  • Segmentation: divide a broad market into smaller groups of buyers with similar needs or characteristics. Common bases are geographic (where), demographic (age, income, gender), psychographic (lifestyle, values), and behavioral (usage, benefits sought, loyalty).
  • Targeting: evaluate the segments and pick which one(s) to serve, based on their size, growth, profitability, and fit with your strengths.
  • Positioning: decide how you want the target to perceive your brand relative to competitors — the distinct place you occupy in their mind.

Why it matters

Focusing on the right segment lets you tailor your product, message, and price so they resonate deeply, rather than blandly appealing to everyone and exciting no one. Clear positioning makes your brand the obvious choice for the people who matter most.

Positioning tools

A positioning statement sums up the target, the category, the key benefit, and the reason to believe. A perceptual map plots brands on two key attributes (e.g., price vs. quality) to reveal gaps and crowding.

Key takeaways

  • You can't serve everyone — segment, then choose.
  • Segment by geography, demographics, psychographics, or behavior.
  • Pick targets by attractiveness and fit.
  • Position your brand on a clear, valued difference.
  • Points of difference set you apart; points of parity keep you in the running.
intermediate_html:
Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning (STP) is the backbone of strategic marketing. It translates the buyer understanding from Lesson 3 into concrete choices about whom to serve and how to be perceived, and it determines how the marketing mix (Lessons 6–9) will be configured. The logic is sequential: segment the market, target the best segment(s), then position the offering.

Step 1: Segmentation

Market segmentation divides a heterogeneous market into homogeneous subgroups that may warrant distinct offerings. The main bases for consumer markets are: geographic (region, city size, climate, density); demographic (age, gender, income, occupation, education, family life cycle — the most common because it is measurable); psychographic (lifestyle, social class, personality, values); and behavioral (occasions, benefits sought, user status, usage rate, loyalty status — often the most strategically powerful). Business markets add bases such as industry, company size, and purchasing approach. For a segment to be useful, it must satisfy five criteria, often remembered as MASDA: Measurable, Accessible, Substantial (large/profitable enough), Differentiable, and Actionable.

Step 2: Targeting

Targeting evaluates each segment on attractiveness (size, growth, profitability, competitive intensity, structural attractiveness via Porter's forces) and fit with company objectives and resources. The firm then chooses a coverage strategy: undifferentiated (mass marketing, one offer for all); differentiated (separate offers for several segments); concentrated/niche (one segment served intensively — ideal for limited resources); or micromarketing (local or individual customization). The choice trades reach against tailoring and cost.

Step 3: Positioning

Positioning is the act of designing the company's offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the minds of target customers (Ries & Trout, 1981). It is anchored on points of difference (PODs) — attributes the brand owns that customers value and competitors lack — and points of parity (POPs) — attributes shared with competitors that are necessary to be considered a legitimate option. A formal positioning statement follows the template: To [target], [brand] is the [frame of reference] that [point of difference] because [reason to believe].

Perceptual mapping

A perceptual (positioning) map plots competing brands on two axes representing key buying criteria (e.g., price vs. performance). It visually reveals competitive clusters, the brand's current position, and open "white spaces" — unmet combinations that may signal opportunity (if backed by real demand).

Worked mini-case

A new gym chain segments the fitness market behaviorally and psychographically and identifies four segments: budget-conscious casual users, time-pressed professionals, hardcore athletes, and social/community seekers. Evaluating attractiveness, it finds time-pressed professionals are substantial, growing, and underserved (passing MASDA). It targets them with a concentrated strategy. Its positioning: "To busy urban professionals, FitFast is the gym that delivers a complete workout in 30 minutes through express, trainer-guided circuits, because our scientifically designed stations eliminate wait time." On a perceptual map of "time efficiency vs. price," FitFast claims the high-efficiency/mid-price white space. The 4Ps then flow logically: compact express equipment (Product), premium-but-justified membership (Price), CBD locations near offices (Place), and "fit fitness into your day" messaging (Promotion).

How to apply it

  • Choose segmentation bases that predict differences in buying behavior, not just describe people.
  • Test each segment against MASDA before targeting it.
  • Pick a coverage strategy matched to your resources — niche beats spread-thin for smaller firms.
  • Write a single, sharp positioning statement and let it drive every mix decision.
  • Use a perceptual map to find defensible, demand-backed white space.

STP is the strategic hinge: it converts buyer insight (Lesson 3) into the targeted execution of branding (Lesson 5) and the full marketing mix.
full_html:
Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning (STP) constitutes the strategic core of modern marketing — the discipline by which a firm decides where to compete and how to win. It is the essential bridge between analysis (the environment and buyer behavior of Lessons 2–3) and action (the marketing mix of Lessons 5–9). The premise, articulated since Smith's (1956) seminal distinction between product differentiation and market segmentation, is that markets are heterogeneous and that aligning a focused offering to a well-chosen segment delivers superior value and profitability than undifferentiated mass marketing.

Step 1: Market segmentation

Segmentation partitions a heterogeneous market into internally homogeneous, externally heterogeneous subgroups that may merit distinct marketing programs. The principal bases for consumer markets (Kotler & Keller, 2016) are: geographic (nation, region, city size, density, climate); demographic (age, life-cycle stage, gender, income, occupation, education, religion, generation — popular because variables are easy to measure and often correlate with needs); psychographic (lifestyle, values, personality, social class — e.g., the VALS typology); and behavioral (occasions, benefits sought, user and loyalty status, usage rate, buyer-readiness stage, attitude). Many scholars argue benefit segmentation (Haley, 1968) and behavioral bases are the most strategically meaningful because they segment on the cause of purchase (the benefit pursued) rather than mere description. Business-market segmentation adds demographic firm variables, operating variables, purchasing approaches, situational factors, and personal characteristics (Bonoma & Shapiro, 1983).

For a segmentation scheme to be actionable, segments must satisfy criteria commonly summarized as MASDA: Measurable (size, purchasing power, and characteristics can be quantified), Accessible (reachable and serviceable), Substantial (large and profitable enough to justify a tailored program), Differentiable (segments respond differently to different mixes), and Actionable (the firm can design effective programs to attract and serve them). Methodologically, segments are increasingly derived empirically via cluster analysis, latent-class models, and — for needs-based segmentation tied to product attributes — conjoint analysis (linked to Lesson 2 research methods).

Step 2: Targeting

Targeting evaluates and selects segments. Attractiveness is assessed on segment size and growth, structural attractiveness (Porter's five forces — rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, buyer and supplier power), and the firm's objectives and resources; a large, fast-growing segment is unattractive if it is fiercely competitive or misaligned with capabilities. The firm then selects a coverage strategy (Kotler & Keller, 2016): undifferentiated (mass) marketing ignores segment differences with one offer — cost-efficient but vulnerable to focused rivals; differentiated marketing designs distinct offers for multiple segments — raising sales but also costs; concentrated (niche) marketing pursues a large share of one or few segments — capital-efficient and ideal for resource-constrained firms, though risky if the niche shrinks or attracts a giant; and micromarketing (local and individual/one-to-one marketing) tailors to local tastes or individuals, enabled by data and digital personalization. The selection embodies a fundamental trade-off between economies of scale (favoring breadth) and relevance/fit (favoring focus).

Step 3: Positioning and differentiation

Positioning, in the foundational formulation of Ries and Trout (1981), is "not what you do to a product… [but] what you do to the mind of the prospect" — the act of designing the offering and image to occupy a distinctive and valued place in target customers' minds. It rests on the analysis of points of parity (POPs) and points of difference (PODs) (Keller, 2008). PODs are strong, favorable, unique brand associations that customers value and believe the brand delivers better than rivals; they should be desirable (to the consumer) and deliverable (by the firm). POPs are associations not unique to the brand but necessary — category POPs establish legitimacy as a member of the category, and competitive POPs negate competitors' claimed PODs. Effective positioning achieves the necessary parity while owning a defensible difference. The discipline is captured in a positioning statement: To [target segment and need], [brand] is the [competitive frame of reference] that [statement of point of difference] because [reason(s) to believe]. Treacy and Wiersema's (1995) value disciplines — operational excellence, product leadership, customer intimacy — offer a complementary strategic frame for choosing a positioning axis.

Perceptual mapping

A perceptual map spatially represents customer perceptions of competing brands along two (or more) salient determinant attributes — for instance, price versus quality, or functionality versus ease of use. Constructed from survey data (often via multidimensional scaling or factor analysis), it reveals competitive clusters, the brand's perceived position relative to the ideal point of the target, and potential white spaces. A crucial caveat: an empty region indicates an unoccupied position, which is an opportunity only if latent demand exists there; many "gaps" are empty precisely because customers do not want that combination (e.g., expensive-and-low-quality).

Worked example

A premium audio startup researches the headphone market and, via cluster analysis on benefit and behavioral data, identifies five segments. It scores each on attractiveness (size, growth, margin) and fit. The "audiophile commuter" segment is substantial, growing, high-margin, and aligned with the firm's engineering strength — passing all MASDA tests. The firm targets it with a concentrated strategy. Positioning statement: "To audiophile commuters who refuse to compromise sound for portability, AuralOne is the wireless headphone that delivers studio-grade noise-cancelled audio in a foldable form, because our proprietary driver and adaptive ANC are tuned by Grammy-winning engineers." PODs: studio-grade sound + best-in-class ANC; category POPs: comfort, battery life, Bluetooth reliability (table-stakes that must be matched). On a perceptual map of "audio fidelity vs. portability," AuralOne claims the high-fidelity/high-portability quadrant where rivals are sparse. The mix follows: premium driver design (Product), skim/premium pricing justified by the POD (Price), specialty audio retailers and DTC (Place), and demonstration-led, credibility-anchored communications (Promotion) — illustrating how positioning disciplines every downstream decision.

Common misconceptions and debates

  • Segmentation as description vs. prediction. Demographic segments are easy to measure but may not predict behavior; the strongest schemes segment on benefits sought and behavior (Haley, 1968). "Women aged 25–34" is a description, not a need-based segment.
  • Over-segmentation. Slicing a market too finely yields segments that fail the Substantial test, fragmenting effort and eroding scale economies. The "segment of one" is feasible only where personalization is cheap (digital).
  • Positioning is not a slogan. A tagline communicates positioning; it is not the positioning itself, which lives in customers' minds and must be earned through the entire mix.
  • White-space fallacy. An empty perceptual-map region may reflect absent demand, not opportunity; gaps must be validated against real willingness to pay.
  • Repositioning risk. Established positions are sticky; attempts to reposition can confuse the market and alienate the existing base (e.g., when a value brand chases premium customers). Positioning consistency builds the brand equity developed in Lesson 5.

Cross-links and synthesis

STP is the strategic hinge of the syllabus. It draws its raw material from Consumer & Business Buyer Behavior (Lesson 3) and the research and environmental analysis of Lesson 2; it directly determines the brand identity and equity built in Branding & Brand Equity (Lesson 5); and it dictates the configuration of the entire marketing mix — product strategy (Lesson 6), pricing (Lesson 7), channels (Lesson 8), and communications (Lesson 9). Strategic tools such as the Ansoff matrix (market penetration vs. development) and the BCG matrix interact with targeting decisions about where to grow. The unifying principle: sustainable advantage comes not from serving everyone, but from choosing the right customers and owning a valued, defensible position in their minds.
